title: Choosing a Git Workflow for a Shared Automation Script
choices: Create a feature branch, commit your changes, open a pull request for review, then merge
Commit directly to main and push whenever you have working code
Email the updated .py file to the team and have them copy-paste it in manually
Force-push your local main over origin/main so everyone gets your version

problem.statement: Three network engineers share one Ansible/Netmiko automation repo that pushes config changes to production switches. You need to add a new NTP-check task without risking someone else's in-progress work or breaking main. What is the correct Git workflow?
problem.use_case: Automation scripts that touch production network config need the same review discipline as application code — an unreviewed direct push to main can misconfigure hundreds of devices the next time the pipeline runs.

hints: Think about how code review and CI checks get applied before something reaches main.
Isolating your work in its own branch protects both your changes and everyone else's until they're reviewed.
Feature branch -> pull request -> review -> merge is the standard collaborative workflow.

acceptable_answers: create a feature branch, commit your changes, open a pull request for review, then merge
solution.explanation: A feature branch keeps your work isolated until it's reviewed and merged via a pull request, giving teammates and CI a chance to catch issues before they reach main and get deployed to production devices.
